answer:
**Charles Comte's strongest formulation** appears in his rejection of climate as a cause of species (or racial) traits, which he says remain fixed across all environments: "The action of the atmosphere, so powerful on the individual, is without influence on his posterity. Children of the Caucasian species are born white in all latitudes... Not only does the influence of climate not affect the color of the species, but it does not even affect the entire individual."[^1] He concedes climate *might* modulate individual vigor, intellect, or passions without altering species essence: "although climate produces no deviation in the species, one can conceive that it could diminish or increase the physical forces of individuals, weaken or strengthen their intellectual faculties, irritate or calm their passions."[^2]

**Charles Dunoyer's strongest formulation** echoes Comte on racial fixity—"the diverse varieties... invariably conserve the characters that are proper to them. These characters remain the same in all latitudes and in all climates"—before pivoting to critique excessive emphasis on milieu.[^3] He rejects climate as origin of traits or even major driver of national differences, prioritizing race: "one must not... attribute to climate the development of the European race."[^4]

**Agreement**: Both insist climate cannot produce or alter fixed racial/species characters (e.g., color, features, stature), which persist unchanged across environments. Peoples retain "national character" markers independently of latitude.[^1][^3]

**Disagreement**: Dunoyer dedicates a full chapter to *external circumstances* (climate chief among them) as real but secondary shapers of development/liberty, modifiable by man ("cultivation has changed even the climate").[^5] Comte, however, would likely see this as overweighting climate's *ongoing* force on traits like vigor or intellect (which Dunoyer explores extensively), since Comte reserves such effects for individuals only, not peoples or "national character," and subordinates them to innate species limits.[^2]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [ch.4/paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/04-des-causes-physiques-auxquelles-a-ete-attribuee-la/translation/#p-78b1b2).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [ch.5/paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/05-de-l-influence-attribuee-a-l-action-des-climats-su/translation/#p-b866a8).
[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.2/paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-d3fec5).
[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.2/paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-ebe80b).
[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.3/passages](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la-/translation/#p-405b54-p-0446c9).
